title: Forgiving others part 2
scripture_text: ‘’Jesus said ‘For if you forgive other people when they sin against you, your heavenly Father will also forgive you. But if you do not forgive others their sins, your Father will not forgive your sins.’
devotional:
These word are a footnote or an appendix to the Lord’s prayer. They are a commentary on verse 12 which reads, And forgive us our debts as we also have forgiven our debtors.

So what is Jesus saying?

1.Those who are forgiven by God should forgive others.

As we have already said, forgiving others can be very difficult, painful and demanding, especially if we have been severely hurt or traumatised by them. Forgiveness can feel like a step too far. Yet we are confronted by these words of Jesus.

We need to look at who Jesus is talking to here. He is not talking to those who are needing initial, first time forgiveness for entry into relationship with God. These people were already clearly believers in Jesus' mind. These are people that possess the kingdom of heaven (5:3, 10), who hunger and thirst after righteousness (6), who are called the sons of God (9), who are the salt of the earth (13) and the light of the world (14) and who have God as their personal Father in a relational way.

These are people who have experienced and enjoyed the enriching freeing power of the grace of God; they are people who have learned that they can be redefined by forgiveness. They are people who have had their shame removed. So for them not to show forgiveness is to belittle what God has done in their lives and deny who they have become in Jesus.

As Christians, God expects us to be the most forgiving people.

In the New Testament, the word forgiveness was used primarily to describe the release of someone from a financial obligation. Forgiveness gives up the right to hurt back. When you forgive someone, you are saying, “What that person did to me was wrong. He has hurt me deeply and deserves to pay for his offence. But today I am releasing him of the obligation he has toward me. I am not forgiving him because he has asked to be forgiven or deserves to be forgiven. I am forgiving him because of the tremendous forgiveness God has offered me.’’

Asking for God’s forgiveness for one’s own sins, all the while withholding forgiveness from someone else, is not only bizarre but hypocritical.

We forgive because our relationships are important to our Father.

One of the things God will hold us accountable for is the nature and quality of our relationships. Imagine two children sitting in the back of the car arguing, and mum and dad are in the front of the car, overjoyed, thrilled and delighted that the children are arguing and fighting. Of course, this would not be the case. When I have an unforgiving heart towards others, I am internalising my conflict with them. We can weaponise our emotions, and begin to harbour feelings of resentment and bitterness.

Sadly the church has created a consumer approach to relationships. I get involved with others if they meet my needs, and if they hurt me then I am free to walk away, my relationship with them is like a ‘soiled nappy,’ disposable. I don’t have to work through the issues and the struggles of forgiving.

We are never more like God than when we forgive.

The glories of the character of God is most clearly seen when we have a heart of forgiveness. The life of Jesus is best displayed through our love and forgiveness of others. When we forgive another person, we discover that we are participating in a supernatural event. God not only gives the command to forgive, but the power to forgive. Conversely, when we abstain from the difficult process of forgiving and choose not to forgive, then we are most unlike God.
prayer: Father may the attitudes of my heart echo your heart.
html:
Forgiving others part 2

Study Text: Matthew 6:14-15
‘’Jesus said ‘For if you forgive other people when they sin against you, your heavenly Father will also forgive you. But if you do not forgive others their sins, your Father will not forgive your sins.’

These word are a footnote or an appendix to the Lord’s prayer. They are a commentary on verse 12 which reads, And forgive us our debts as we also have forgiven our debtors.

So what is Jesus saying?

1.Those who are forgiven by God should forgive others.

As we have already said, forgiving others can be very difficult, painful and demanding, especially if we have been severely hurt or traumatised by them. Forgiveness can feel like a step too far. Yet we are confronted by these words of Jesus.

We need to look at who Jesus is talking to here. He is not talking to those who are needing initial, first time forgiveness for entry into relationship with God. These people were already clearly believers in Jesus' mind. These are people that possess the kingdom of heaven (5:3, 10), who hunger and thirst after righteousness (6), who are called the sons of God (9), who are the salt of the earth (13) and the light of the world (14) and who have God as their personal Father in a relational way.

These are people who have experienced and enjoyed the enriching freeing power of the grace of God; they are people who have learned that they can be redefined by forgiveness. They are people who have had their shame removed. So for them not to show forgiveness is to belittle what God has done in their lives and deny who they have become in Jesus.

As Christians, God expects us to be the most forgiving people.

In the New Testament, the word forgiveness was used primarily to describe the release of someone from a financial obligation. Forgiveness gives up the right to hurt back. When you forgive someone, you are saying, “What that person did to me was wrong. He has hurt me deeply and deserves to pay for his offence. But today I am releasing him of the obligation he has toward me. I am not forgiving him because he has asked to be forgiven or deserves to be forgiven. I am forgiving him because of the tremendous forgiveness God has offered me.’’

Asking for God’s forgiveness for one’s own sins, all the while withholding forgiveness from someone else, is not only bizarre but hypocritical.

We forgive because our relationships are important to our Father.

One of the things God will hold us accountable for is the nature and quality of our relationships. Imagine two children sitting in the back of the car arguing, and mum and dad are in the front of the car, overjoyed, thrilled and delighted that the children are arguing and fighting. Of course, this would not be the case. When I have an unforgiving heart towards others, I am internalising my conflict with them. We can weaponise our emotions, and begin to harbour feelings of resentment and bitterness.

Sadly the church has created a consumer approach to relationships. I get involved with others if they meet my needs, and if they hurt me then I am free to walk away, my relationship with them is like a ‘soiled nappy,’ disposable. I don’t have to work through the issues and the struggles of forgiving.

We are never more like God than when we forgive.

The glories of the character of God is most clearly seen when we have a heart of forgiveness. The life of Jesus is best displayed through our love and forgiveness of others. When we forgive another person, we discover that we are participating in a supernatural event. God not only gives the command to forgive, but the power to forgive. Conversely, when we abstain from the difficult process of forgiving and choose not to forgive, then we are most unlike God.

Prayer: Father may the attitudes of my heart echo your heart.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Psalms 18-19
